## Highlights from "The Art of Doing Science and Engineering" by Richard Hamming

> Location 312 | Added on Thursday, January 1, 2026

Most people spend their careers working on problems that don't really matter. They choose safe, incremental topics where they can guarantee results rather than risking failure on something significant. The crucial question to keep asking yourself is: what are the important problems in my field, and why am I not working on one of them?

---

> Location 487 | Added on Thursday, January 1, 2026

Having a vision is not the same as having a plan. A vision tells you where you want to end up; a plan tells you the next step. Too many scientists have elaborate plans but no overarching vision, so they execute flawlessly on work that leads nowhere in particular. The vision must come first, and the plan must serve it — never the reverse.

---

> Location 623 | Added on Thursday, January 1, 2026

Style is not a superficial matter in scientific work. The way you present an idea shapes whether anyone absorbs it. An elegant proof does more than a correct one, because elegance reveals the underlying structure and makes the result usable. Clarity of presentation is clarity of thought — they cannot be separated.

---

> Location 741 | Added on Thursday, January 1, 2026

If you do not work on important problems, it is not likely that you will do important work. It seems obvious, yet so many talented people fritter away their potential on trivial questions. The courage to tackle what matters is itself a kind of ability, one that can be cultivated but rarely is.

---

> Location 889 | Added on Thursday, January 1, 2026

You must learn to sell your ideas. Many scientists believe the work should speak for itself, but this is a delusion. If you want your work to have impact, you must learn to communicate it — to frame it so others can see its importance. Great work that nobody knows about might as well not exist.

---
